sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

1998.assessment: GDP per capita near $720 in 1998, close to the pre-crisis peak. Côte d'Ivoire remained the largest economy in Francophone West Africa. Political tensions around Ivoirité and Ouattara's citizenship eligibility grew but had not yet disrupted economic activity.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

2001.assessment: GDP per capita approximately $590 in 2001. Gbagbo government struggling with ethnic tensions and economic stagnation. Reconciliation Forum held but fundamental political divisions remained. Investment climate deteriorating.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

2003.assessment: GDP per capita approximately $550 in 2003, reflecting the severe impact of civil war. Country partitioned with Forces Nouvelles in north. Linas-Marcoussis Agreement signed January 2003 but fighting continued. Economic activity in conflict zones collapsed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: World Bank WDI NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Cote d'Ivoire

2010 would have frozen new investment decisions but the annual figure reflects
mostly H1 activity.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2010

2011.assessment:
FDI turned negative (approximately -0.5% of GDP) in 2011 — a net capital
outflow. The civil war caused significant business flight, banking closure,
and disinvestment. International companies withdrew staff and assets.
Post-crisis H2 2011 saw initial re-engagement but the full-year figure
remained negative.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2011

2012.assessment:
FDI recovered strongly to approximately 1.8% of GDP in 2012. HIPC debt
relief, political stabilization, and strong GDP growth attracted investors.
French, Lebanese, and other international investors returned. Telecom,
agribusiness, construction sectors attracting most investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2012

2013.assessment:
FDI rose to approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2013, the highest since the civil war
period. International confidence in the Ouattara government's economic program
attracted substantial investment. Mining, infrastructure, agribusiness, and
financial services sectors growing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2013

2014.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2014, maintaining the elevated post-recovery
levels. Continued strong growth and security consolidation attracting investment.
Côte d'Ivoire becoming the most attractive FDI destination in Francophone
West Africa.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2014

2015.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2015. Stable at prior year levels. Global FDI
flows to Africa somewhat reduced by commodity price declines but Côte d'Ivoire
maintained investor confidence. Infrastructure and cocoa processing attracting
continued investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2015

2016.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.1% of GDP in 2016. UN sanctions lifted (April 2016) removed
a remaining constraint on investment. Military mutiny in January 2016 (actually
January 2017) and cocoa price decline did not significantly deter investment.
Infrastructure PPP projects attracting private capital.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2016

2017.assessment:
FDI approximately 2.0% of GDP in 2017. Military mutinies in January and May
temporarily dampened sentiment but the rapid resolution through cash payments
restored confidence. Cocoa sector price volatility limited agribusiness
investment. ONUCI withdrawal seen as a sign of stability.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2017

2018.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.8% of GDP in 2018. Slightly moderated from peak years
as the post-conflict investment surge normalized. Succession uncertainty
for 2020 election beginning to create some hesitation. IMF program maintaining
macro stability which continued to attract foreign investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2018

2019.assessment:
FDI approximately 1.7% of GDP in 2019. Continued modest decline from peak.
Political succession uncertainty for 2020 election creating some investor
caution. Sahel security spillover risk emerging. Services sector FDI
growing while manufacturing remained limited.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2019

2020.assessment:
FDI declined to approximately 1.5% of GDP in 2020. COVID-19 global FDI
decline affected Côte d'Ivoire. Post-election political uncertainty further
dampened investment. Despite challenges, economy remained resilient and
investor confidence was maintained relative to peers.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2020

2021.assessment:
FDI recovered slightly to approximately 1.6% of GDP in 2021. Post-COVID
recovery and reconciliation process improved investor confidence. Strong
economic rebound (~7%) attracted renewed interest. Services and infrastructure
sectors leading investment recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Cote d'Ivoire 2021

2022.assessment: FDI approximately 2.8% GDP in 2022. Strong investment flows in oil and gas, mining, telecom, and consumer goods. Côte d'Ivoire remained most attractive FDI destination in Francophone West Africa. EU investors dominant.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI FDI data Cote d'Ivoire 2022

2023.assessment: FDI ~2.5% GDP. Continued moderate-to-strong investment flows. Some caution from jihadist spillover in north but overall investor confidence high.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI FDI data Cote d'Ivoire 2023

2024.assessment: FDI estimated ~2.6% GDP. Continued investments in infrastructure, energy, and services.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI FDI data Cote d'Ivoire 2024

2025.assessment: FDI projected ~2.7% GDP. Stable investment climate maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI FDI data Cote d'Ivoire 2025

1996.assessment: No significant foreign military presence. France maintained standard cooperation agreement (accords de defense) but no operational deployments. Full Ivorian sovereignty over territory.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 1996

1997.assessment: Full sovereignty; no foreign military forces. Standard French-Ivorian bilateral defense agreement.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 1997

1998.assessment: Full sovereignty. No foreign military presence beyond routine advisors under bilateral cooperation.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 1998

1999.assessment: Small French military presence; limited advisors. Post-December 1999 coup, French position was neutral. No significant foreign military forces deployed.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 1999

2000.assessment: Limited French military advisors under bilateral agreements. Gbagbo government maintained French-Ivorian defense cooperation despite political tensions.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2000

2001.assessment: Limited French military advisors. Gbagbo increasingly nationalist rhetoric but formal military ties maintained. No operational forces.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2001

2002.assessment: French Operation Licorne deployed September 2002 with approximately 4,000 troops as ceasefire monitoring force creating buffer zone. ECOWAS deployed MICECI forces. Licorne operated under bilateral invitation from Gbagbo initially, became contested later.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2002

2003.assessment: Operation Licorne (French) ~4,000-5,000 troops maintained buffer zone. UN Mission MINUCI deployed May 2003 (~200 military observers). Linas-Marcoussis Agreement authorized presence. Licorne prevented collapse but Gbagbo increasingly hostile to French role.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2003

2004.assessment: ONUCI (UN Operation Côte d'Ivoire) deployed April 2004, eventually up to 9,000 peacekeepers. Licorne ~3,500-4,000 as support force under Chapter VII. November 2004 Ivorian air strikes on Licorne led to crisis - French forces destroyed entire Ivorian air force.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2004

2005.assessment: ONUCI ~8,500 UN peacekeepers; Licorne ~3,500. UNSCR 1643 maintained mandate. Peacekeeping forces held buffer zone between north and south. Elections postponed; Mbeki mediation backed by ONUCI.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2005

2006.assessment: ONUCI ~7,500; Licorne ~3,000. Forces maintained presence despite Gbagbo hostility. Jeunes Patriotes harassed ONUCI personnel. UN-authorized force maintained buffer function under UNSCR 1721.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2006

2007.assessment: ONUCI ~7,000; Licorne ~2,500. OPA signed March 2007 with Guillaume Soro as PM improved operational environment. DDR process began. Forces monitoring disarmament.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2007

2008.assessment: ONUCI ~6,500; Licorne ~2,000. OPA implementation ongoing. DDR process partial. Elections continued to be postponed. Forces maintained stability monitoring function.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2008

2009.assessment:
Two significant foreign military presences in 2009: ONUCI (UN Operation in
Côte d'Ivoire) with approximately 8,000 peacekeepers mandated under UNSC
Resolution 1528 (2004); and French Licorne forces (~1,600 troops) under
bilateral defense agreement supporting ONUCI. Both presences were invited and
authorized by the internationally recognized government framework.

sources.citation: UNOCI mission reports 2009; French Ministry of Defense Licorne

2010.assessment:
ONUCI and French Licorne forces both present in 2010. Following the post-election
crisis in November 2010, both forces were reinforced and their mandates
strengthened. UNSC authorized ONUCI to use all necessary means to protect
civilians. The forces were increasingly aligned with Ouattara's internationally
recognized government.

sources.citation: UNOCI mission reports 2010; UNSC authorization to protect civilians

2011.assessment:
Foreign military presence peaked and was actively engaged in combat in early 2011.
French Licorne forces directly participated in operations against Gbagbo's heavy
weapons and presidential residence (April 2011), explicitly under UNSC Resolution
1975. ONUCI conducted joint operations with FRCI. After Gbagbo's capture,
forces remained but with diminishing combat role.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1975; French MOD Licorne operational reports; ONUCI mission records

2012.assessment:
Foreign military presence remaining at significant level in 2012 but transitioning
to peacekeeping/stabilization role. ONUCI continued with approximately 9,000
peacekeepers. French Licorne reduced to approximately 900 troops. Forces
supporting security sector reform and DDR rather than combat operations.

sources.citation: ONUCI mission reports 2012; French Ministry of Defense Licorne reduction

2013.assessment:
Foreign military presence continued but reducing in 2013. ONUCI mandate
was being reviewed for drawdown timeline. French Licorne further reduced.
Security stabilization allowing planned reduction. Forces supporting
rather than substituting for national security forces.

sources.citation: ONUCI mission reports 2013; UNSC renewal decisions

2014.assessment:
ONUCI peacekeeping mission continued in 2014 but with reducing troop numbers.
French Licorne forces wound down significantly. ONUCI supporting rule of law
and security sector reform activities. Mission expected to conclude by
2017 based on progress trajectory.

sources.citation: ONUCI mission reports 2014; UNSC Resolution 2162

2015.assessment:
ONUCI continuing with reduced mandate in 2015. French Licorne forces
completed withdrawal. Bilateral French defense cooperation (Éléments français
en Côte d'Ivoire - EFCI) replaced Licorne on permanent basis. Peaceful
2015 election reinforced the case for ONUCI withdrawal planning.

sources.citation: ONUCI 2015 reports; French EFCI establishment; UNSC renewal

2016.assessment:
ONUCI in its final year of operations in 2016. Troop numbers reduced to
minimum needed for closeout. French EFCI (permanent bilateral force) continued
at Abidjan base. UN sanctions lifted April 2016 consistent with overall
normalization. ONUCI mandate to expire June 2017.

sources.citation: ONUCI final year reports 2016; UNSC withdrawal decision

2017.assessment:
ONUCI mission formally completed and withdrew in June 2017, ending 13 years
of UN peacekeeping in Côte d'Ivoire. French EFCI bilateral defense presence
remained at Port-Bouët base (~600 troops) as a permanent bilateral arrangement
under the Franco-Ivoirian defense agreement. Military mutinies were a domestic
issue not involving foreign forces.

sources.citation: ONUCI completion June 2017; French EFCI status; UNSC records

2018.assessment:
Foreign military presence reduced to French EFCI bilateral forces only (~600)
in 2018. No UN peacekeeping mission. French forces at Port-Bouët under
bilateral defense treaty. Some US military training cooperation for counterterrorism.
No contested or uninvited foreign military presence.

sources.citation: French EFCI status reports; Franco-Ivoirian bilateral defense agreement

2019.assessment:
Foreign military presence continued as French EFCI bilateral forces in 2019.
Growing US military cooperation for counterterrorism as Sahel jihadist
spillover threat emerged. Côte d'Ivoire participating in regional security
frameworks (G5 Sahel adjacent). All foreign forces invited/bilateral.

sources.citation: French EFCI 2019; US AFRICOM cooperation; bilateral defense

2020.assessment:
French EFCI bilateral military presence continuing in 2020. Kafolo attack
(June 2020) by jihadists killed Ivoirian soldiers, prompting enhanced
French military cooperation for counterterrorism in the north. Election
period saw enhanced internal security but no additional foreign military.

sources.citation: French EFCI; Kafolo attack response; bilateral defense cooperation

2021.assessment:
French EFCI bilateral military presence continuing in 2021. Counterterrorism
cooperation intensified with northern border security threats growing. France
provided training and intelligence support for Ivoirian security forces in the
north. All foreign military presence is invited bilateral forces.

sources.citation: French EFCI 2021; bilateral CT cooperation; northern security

2022.assessment: Small French military presence (~500) as part of ongoing bilateral security cooperation at Port-Bouet airbase. No active peacekeeping mission. ONUCI fully withdrew in 2017. Jihadist threat from Burkina Faso border growing but French presence limited.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2022

2023.assessment: French military contingent (~500 troops) maintained at Abidjan base under bilateral cooperation. Joint patrols in north against jihadist spill-over from Burkina Faso. No major foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2023

2024.assessment: Reduced French military presence as France reviewed Africa strategy post-Sahel withdrawals. Bilateral security cooperation against JNIM/Ansarul Islam threats in north. Ivorian forces leading.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2024

2025.assessment: Continued limited bilateral military cooperation. Ivorian security forces primary counter-terrorism actor in north.

sources.citation: UN Peacekeeping ONUCI records; French Ministry of Defense 2025

1996.assessment: Minimal displacement in 1996. Côte d'Ivoire was a refugee-receiving country for Liberian and Sierra Leonean war refugees but experienced minimal internal displacement or outflows of its own nationals.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 1996
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

1997.assessment: Minimal displacement of Ivorian nationals. Some return of Liberian refugees as Liberia stabilized.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 1997
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

1998.assessment: Minimal displacement. Stable country receiving some regional refugees. No significant Ivorian refugee flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 1998
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

1999.assessment: December 1999 coup caused some displacement and migration uncertainty but no major refugee flows. Some international workers left temporarily.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 1999
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2000.assessment: Some displacement from post-coup political violence. October 2000 elections violence caused localized displacement particularly affecting northern Ivorians (Dioula/Muslim communities).

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2000
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2001.assessment: Continued moderate displacement from intercommunal violence. Some Ivorians moved to safer areas. Total displaced ~100,000 but no mass cross-border flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2001
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2002.assessment: Civil war beginning September 2002 caused significant displacement. ~400,000 IDPs in first months; some cross-border flows to Ghana, Liberia, Guinea, Mali. Partition caused population movement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2002
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2003.assessment: Active civil war caused mass displacement. ~800,000+ IDPs; ~50,000-100,000 refugees in neighboring countries. Northern-southern movement along ethnic lines. Massive humanitarian crisis.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2003
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2004.assessment: Continued mass displacement during ongoing partition. ~750,000 IDPs; refugees in Ghana and Mali (50,000+). ONUCI and humanitarian agencies active.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2004
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2005.assessment: Displacement declining slightly but still massive. ~700,000 IDPs; continued cross-border refugees. Some spontaneous returns attempted.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2005
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2006.assessment: Some reduction in displacement as ceasefire held. ~600,000 IDPs; gradual return movement to north. UNHCR assisted some returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2006
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2007.assessment: Significant improvement following OPA March 2007. Displaced falling to ~400,000. Return movement accelerating. Cross-border refugees beginning to return.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2007
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2008.assessment: Continued return movement. ~200,000 IDPs remaining from civil war; cross-border refugee population declining. OPA implementation allowing returns.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2008
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2009.assessment:
Residual displacement from the 2002-2007 civil war period. Approximately 55,000
Ivoirian refugees remained in Liberia and Ghana from the earlier conflict.
Some internal displacement persisted in contested zones. The north-south
partition under the Ouagadougou Agreement still in effect created ongoing
population movement uncertainty.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2009; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire country report

2010.assessment:
Post-election crisis beginning November 2010 triggered new displacement. An
estimated 30,000-50,000 Ivoirians fled to Liberia in November-December 2010.
Internal displacement in Abidjan and western regions reached approximately
100,000-150,000. Total new displacement approximately 0.8% of the ~19.7M population.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2010; IDMC post-election crisis report

2011.assessment:
The post-election civil war (January-April 2011) caused massive displacement.
Approximately 150,000-200,000 Ivoirians fled as refugees to Liberia (130,000),
Ghana, and other neighbors. Internal displacement peaked at approximately 350,000.
Total displacement estimated at ~550,000, approximately 2.8% of the population.
The western regions (Man, Duékoué) saw some of the worst violence-driven displacement.
After Gbagbo's capture in April, displacement began to reverse but year-end
stock remained very elevated.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2011; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire crisis; OCHA flash appeals

2012.assessment:
Significant returns began in 2012 but many displaced persons remained in
camps in Liberia and Ghana, particularly pro-Gbagbo supporters fearing
reprisals. Cross-border militia attacks from western Liberia caused new
local displacement in western Cote d'Ivoire. Approximately 115,000 Ivoirian
refugees still registered with UNHCR. Net new displacement estimated ~0.6%.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2012; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire return report

2013.assessment:
Continued return movements and declining displacement in 2013. Ivoirian refugee
population in neighboring countries declining from 2011 peak. Residual
displacement from cross-border pro-Gbagbo militia activity in western regions.
UNHCR supporting voluntary repatriation from Liberia and Ghana. Approximately
60,000 registered refugees remaining. Total displacement approximately 0.3%.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2013; repatriation program data

2014.assessment:
Displacement declining further in 2014 as security improved. Voluntary
repatriation program proceeding. Some protracted caseload remaining in
Liberia — some ex-combatants and supporters reluctant to return. Small
ongoing displacement from localized violence in western regions. Approximately
35,000-45,000 registered refugees remaining.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2014; voluntary repatriation data

2015.assessment:
Displacement at low residual levels in 2015. Registered refugee population
declining toward approximately 25,000 in Liberia and Ghana — protracted
cases, not new displacement. Peaceful presidential election in October
2015 did not generate significant new displacement. Crisis-level displacement
effectively resolved.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2015; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire

2016.assessment:
Displacement at minimal baseline levels in 2016. Remaining Ivoirian
refugees are largely protracted cases who have locally integrated in
Liberia or Ghana. Very few new displacements. Country essentially
normalized from a displacement perspective, though UNHCR maintains
a small residual caseload.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2016; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire

2017.assessment:
Displacement at minimal baseline in 2017. Military mutinies in January
and May 2017 did not cause significant civilian displacement — they were
quickly resolved through negotiation. UNHCR repatriation from Liberia
and Ghana continuing but slow due to protracted status. No new crisis
displacement events.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2017; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire

2018.assessment:
Displacement at minimal baseline in 2018. Residual caseload from 2011
crisis declining through gradual repatriation and local integration.
Northern border areas beginning to show new pressures from Sahel jihadist
spillover but no significant displacement yet. Country otherwise stable.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2018; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire

2019.assessment:
Displacement at minimal baseline in 2019. Country hosting approximately
310,000 refugees from neighboring countries (Liberia, Mali, Burkina Faso,
Sierra Leone) — Côte d'Ivoire is a net refugee-receiving country by this
point. Small new displacement from jihadist incursions in the north. Political
succession crisis (Soro exile) did not cause population displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2019; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire

2020.assessment:
New displacement in 2020 driven by two factors: election-related violence
(October-December 2020) causing internal displacement in western and central
regions; and Kafolo jihadist attack (June 2020) killing 14 soldiers and
displacing villages in the northeast. Election boycott protests turned
violent in some areas. Total new displacement approximately 0.4% of population
(~100,000-120,000 people). Some fled to Liberia from election-related violence.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2020; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire 2020 annual report

2021.assessment:
Displacement declining from 2020 peak in 2021. Post-election reconciliation
process allowing return of some 2020-displaced populations. Northern jihadist
threat continuing to cause small-scale displacement in border villages.
Gbagbo's return from ICC detention in June 2021 did not generate displacement.
Côte d'Ivoire continues to host large refugee population (~310,000) from
neighboring conflict-affected countries.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2021; IDMC Cote d'Ivoire 2021

2022.assessment: Minimal displacement from Ivorian conflict. Some new displacement from jihadist attacks in northern border districts adjacent to Burkina Faso. Côte d'Ivoire received refugees from Burkina Faso (small numbers). Overall minimal displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2022
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2023.assessment: Small increase in displacement from jihadist attacks in north. ~30,000 IDPs from northern border insecurity. Receiving ~15,000-20,000 Burkinabè refugees. Overall stable and manageable.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2023
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2024.assessment: Gradual increase in northern border displacement as jihadist attacks continue. ~40,000 IDPs estimated. Overall limited compared to civil war period.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2024
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

2025.assessment: Growing displacement from northern jihadist activity. ~50,000 IDPs estimated. Some concern about trajectory but still far below crisis levels.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends and IDMC data Cote d'Ivoire 2025
Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC)

1996.assessment: No international sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 1996. Country under Bédié maintained normal international relations.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 1996

1997.assessment: No sanctions. Normal international engagement under Bédié government.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 1997

1998.assessment: No sanctions. Côte d'Ivoire maintained normal international standing.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 1998

1999.assessment: No formal sanctions despite December 1999 coup. EU and donors suspended some aid but no UNSC sanctions imposed.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 1999

2000.assessment: No UN sanctions. Some bilateral aid suspended post-coup but EU gradually resumed engagement after October 2000 elections.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2000

2001.assessment: No sanctions. International community cautiously re-engaged with Gbagbo government.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2001

2002.assessment: No formal UN sanctions despite civil war eruption in September 2002. International community focused on mediation.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2002

2003.assessment: No formal UNSC sanctions in 2003 despite ongoing civil war. Linas-Marcoussis Agreement framework maintained diplomatic engagement.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2003

2004.assessment: UNSC Resolution 1572 (November 2004) imposed arms embargo and authorized targeted sanctions on individuals threatening peace process. EU suspended development assistance. Triggered by November 2004 Ivorian air strikes on French Licorne forces (9 soldiers killed) and subsequent anti-French riots.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2004

2005.assessment: UNSC maintained arms embargo under UNSCR 1572 and renewed via UNSCR 1643 (Dec 2005). Travel bans and asset freezes on individuals threatening peace. Some targeted sanctions applied to specific rebel and government figures.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2005

2006.assessment: UN arms embargo continued under UNSCR 1721 (Nov 2006). Targeted sanctions on specific individuals. EU partially resumed development aid. Sanctions regime maintained pressure on all parties.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2006

2007.assessment: UN sanctions maintained under UNSCR 1782 (Oct 2007) despite OPA peace progress. Arms embargo continued. Some targeted sanctions individuals removed as peace process advanced.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2007

2008.assessment: UN sanctions regime continued under UNSCR 1842 (Oct 2008). Arms embargo maintained. OPA implementation ongoing. Sanctions provided leverage for peace process compliance.

sources.citation: UN Security Council Resolutions on Cote d'Ivoire 2008

2009.assessment:
UN arms embargo on Côte d'Ivoire remained in place in 2009 (UNSC Resolution
1572/2004 extended through renewals). Targeted individual sanctions (asset
freezes, travel bans) applied to specific individuals who undermined the peace
process. The embargo covered both government forces and armed groups. Both
the Gbagbo government and Forces Nouvelles were constrained by the arms
embargo.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1842 (2008) and renewals; UN Sanctions Committee 1572

2010.assessment:
UN arms embargo and targeted individual sanctions continued in 2010. The post-
election crisis in November-December 2010 led the UN and international community
to strengthen sanctions pressure against Gbagbo's regime. EU and US applied
additional targeted financial sanctions against Gbagbo and his inner circle
refusing to transfer power.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1962 (2010) extending sanctions; EU targeted measures against Gbagbo

2011.assessment:
Sanctions continued and intensified in early 2011. UNSC Resolution 1975
(March 2011) explicitly authorized ONUCI to use all necessary means to protect
civilians and effectively authorized the French Licorne intervention against
Gbagbo forces. After Gbagbo's capture in April 2011, sanctions regime pivoted
to support the Ouattara government and wind down the emergency measures.
Arms embargo remained but targeted sanctions against Ouattara-aligned actors
were removed.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 1975 (2011); UN sanctions modifications post-Gbagbo capture

2012.assessment:
Arms embargo and targeted sanctions maintained in 2012 as the transition
consolidated. The UNSC renewed the measures while the Ouattara government
established control. Some individuals had sanctions lifted as they cooperated
with the new government. Gbagbo in ICC custody; his inner circle in exile
or arrested.

sources.citation: UN Sanctions Committee 1572 renewal decisions 2012

2013.assessment:
Sanctions gradually reduced in 2013. UNSC Resolution 2101 (2013) partially
modified the arms embargo to allow government forces to obtain arms. The
targeted individual sanctions list was being reduced. The international
community signaling support for Ouattara's consolidation through gradual
lifting of restrictions.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 2101 (2013) partial arms embargo modification

2014.assessment:
Remaining targeted sanctions continued in 2014 for a small number of
individuals associated with Gbagbo regime and militia leaders. The arms
embargo was effectively loosened for government forces. Formal full lifting
was still pending stability assessment.

sources.citation: UN Sanctions Committee 1572 decisions 2014

2015.assessment:
Sanctions continued to be wound down in 2015. UNSC Resolution 2219 (2015)
further modified the regime. Presidential election in October 2015 passed
without major incident, strengthening the case for full sanctions removal.
The final formal lifting was set for 2016.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 2219 (2015) continued modification of sanctions

2016.assessment:
UN sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire were formally terminated in 2016. UNSC
Resolution 2283 (April 2016) ended the arms embargo and targeted measures
established under Resolution 1572 (2004). This marked the formal conclusion
of the post-civil war sanctions regime. Côte d'Ivoire returned to no-sanctions
status.

sources.citation: UNSC Resolution 2283 (April 2016) terminating Cote d'Ivoire sanctions

2017.assessment:
No sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2017. Fully normalized international status.
Military mutinies did not trigger new sanctions. ONUCI peacekeeping mission
completed and withdrew June 2017. Country regarded as a successful post-
conflict stabilization case by international community.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records 2017; no Cote d'Ivoire sanctions active

2018.assessment:
No sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2018. Fully normalized international
relations. IMF program and World Bank engagement affirmed the international
community's positive view of the country's trajectory.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records 2018

2019.assessment:
No sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2019. Gbagbo's ICC acquittal in January
2019 did not trigger any new measures. Political competition around succession
did not prompt international sanction reactions. Country remained in good
international standing.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records 2019

2020.assessment:
No formal UN sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2020 despite the third-term
electoral crisis. The international community (AU, ECOWAS, UN) expressed
concern about post-election violence but did not impose sanctions.
Ouattara retained bilateral support from France, EU, and US. ECOWAS
called for restraint but did not sanction.

sources.citation: UN Security Council records 2020; ECOWAS statements

2021.assessment:
No sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2021. Political reconciliation process
following the 2020 electoral crisis. Gbagbo's return from ICC detention
in June 2021 was welcomed by international partners as a positive step.
No measures triggered by the post-2020 political environment.

2022.assessment: No international sanctions on Côte d'Ivoire in 2022. All UN sanctions were lifted by UNSCR 2283 in April 2016. Country fully integrated in international community under Ouattara.

2009.assessment:
Freedom House rated Côte d'Ivoire's Civil Liberties at 6 (raw scale 1-7,
7=least free) in 2009, reflecting Not Free status. De facto partition of
the country, presence of armed groups controlling northern regions, arbitrary
detention of political opponents, and constrained press freedom all
contributed to the severe rating.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2010, Cote d'Ivoire

2010.assessment:
Civil Liberties rating remained 6/7 (Not Free) in 2010. Post-election
crisis from November led to severe human rights violations: security forces
attacking civilians, clashes between FDS and FRCI in cities, arbitrary arrests.
Political speech severely constrained with election dispute. Worst civil
liberties environment in recent memory.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2011, Cote d'Ivoire

2011.assessment:
Civil Liberties rating was 6/7 (Not Free) in 2011. The civil war created severe
human rights abuses by both sides — FRCI and FDS. Massacres documented in
Duékoué (west). Post-war period saw revenge killings and impunity for
Ouattara-allied forces. Despite political change, structural civil liberties
conditions remained very poor.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2012, Cote d'Ivoire

2012.assessment:
Civil Liberties improved to 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2012, reflecting the
post-war normalization under Ouattara. Media space improved marginally.
Some political prisoners released. However, pro-Gbagbo figures remained
detained and impunity for FRCI violence was widespread. Classification
moved from Not Free to Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2013, Cote d'Ivoire

2013.assessment:
Civil Liberties maintained at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2013. Media pluralism
developing with some private outlets. Opposition political activity more
tolerated. Security forces impunity for 2011-era crimes remained a major
concern. Reconciliation process for civil war wounds progressing slowly.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2014, Cote d'Ivoire

2014.assessment:
Civil Liberties at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2014. Gradual normalization continuing.
Civil society organizations growing. Media space improved but self-censorship
common. Gbagbo ICC trial proceedings creating political attention. Freedom of
assembly improved from civil war period.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2015, Cote d'Ivoire

2015.assessment:
Civil Liberties at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2015. Presidential election held
with some improvement in political rights. Opposition boycott of October 2015
election reduced competitive tension but also civil liberties pressures.
Press freedom improving; internet access growing. Unresolved impunity for
civil war abuses a continuing concern.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2016, Cote d'Ivoire

2016.assessment:
Civil Liberties at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2016. Relatively stable conditions.
New constitution created Senate but concentrated presidential power. Civil
society active. Media pluralism with some government pressure. New constitution
raised questions about civil liberties provisions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2017, Cote d'Ivoire

2017.assessment:
Civil Liberties at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2017. Military mutinies did not
directly affect civil liberties conditions for the general public. Some
arrests of opposition figures. Journalist safety issues from northern security
deterioration. ONUCI withdrawal meant reduced international monitoring.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2018, Cote d'Ivoire

2018.assessment:
Civil Liberties at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2018. Stable conditions. Growing
civil society. Media with both state and private outlets. Political opposition
more engaged. Succession discussions did not immediately affect liberties.
Increasing internet penetration enabling more information access.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2019, Cote d'Ivoire

2019.assessment:
Civil Liberties improved to 4/7 (Partly Free) in 2019, reflecting continued
gradual improvement in press freedom, civil society space, and legal rights
protections. Gbagbo acquittal by ICC created political space. Opposition
parties more actively engaged. Some positive structural reforms.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2020, Cote d'Ivoire

2020.assessment:
Civil Liberties deteriorated to 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2020. Third-term
candidacy generated significant rights violations: opposition leaders arrested,
post-election violence against protesters, media intimidation. The October
election boycott did not prevent violence and crackdown. Civil liberties
regressed from 2019 improvement.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World 2021, Cote d'Ivoire

2021.assessment:
Civil Liberties remained at 5/7 (Partly Free) in 2021. Gbagbo's return from
ICC detention in June 2021 changed the political landscape. Reconciliation
process beginning. Some opposition prisoners released. Post-election political
crackdown gradually easing. Northern security concerns limiting civil liberties
in affected areas.

sources.citation: ICG

1997.assessment: Bédié dominant; RDR/Ouattara excluded. Elite fragmentation growing.

sources.citation: ICG

1998.assessment: Growing elite tensions; military faction unhappy with Bédié.

sources.citation: ICG

1999.assessment: Military coup December 1999. Guéï junta in power. Elite completely fragmented: PDCI expelled, FPI and RDR competing. Active power struggles.

sources.citation: ICG

2000.assessment: Gbagbo wins elections; RDR/Ouattara excluded. Three-way elite fragmentation (FPI/Gbagbo, RDR/Ouattara, PDCI/Bédié). Active power struggles.

sources.citation: ICG

2001.assessment: Gbagbo government; reconciliation forum. Significant elite competition managed uneasily.

sources.citation: ICG

2002.assessment: Civil war; elite completely fragmented. FN vs Gbagbo; Young Patriots vs FN; multiple armed factions. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: ICG

2003.assessment: Continued fragmentation. Linas-Marcoussis unity government nominally formed but actual fragmentation continues.

sources.citation: ICG

2004.assessment: Fragmented; active power struggles. Gbagbo, FN/Soro, Young Patriots, MPIGO etc. all with distinct interests.

sources.citation: ICG

2005.assessment: Active power struggles; Gbagbo trying to hold together southern coalition vs FN vs PDCI vs RDR.

sources.citation: ICG

2006.assessment: Active power struggles; stalemate between Gbagbo south and FN north.

sources.citation: ICG

2007.assessment: OPA; Gbagbo and Soro (FN) formally aligned as PM. Fragmentation reduced; significant competition managed under OPA framework.

sources.citation: ICG

2008.assessment: OPA implementation; Gbagbo-Soro alliance. Pre-election maneuvering. Managed but significant competition. Opposition (Ouattara/Bédié coalition) forming.

sources.citation: ICG

2009.assessment:
Elite cohesion was defined by deep structural fragmentation in 2009.
The country had two effectively separate ruling elites: Gbagbo's FPI-aligned
southern government and Forces Nouvelles leadership (Guillaume Soro) in the
north. Within the south, divisions between Gbagbo loyalists and those who
favored negotiated settlement were significant. The Ouattara RDR was in
political opposition, participating in government coalitions but marginalized.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports, Cote d'Ivoire 2009; qualitative country analysis

2010.assessment:
Elite cohesion reached its most fractured point in 2010. Post-election crisis
created complete elite rupture: Gbagbo's southern government vs. Ouattara's
internationally recognized government backed by Forces Nouvelles. Within each
camp, internal tensions existed (Guillaume Soro as FN commander aligned with
Ouattara). No coherent national elite existed; two parallel power structures
in open conflict.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; UN reports on Cote d'Ivoire crisis 2010

2011.assessment:
Elite cohesion in 2011 was defined by the defeat of one faction and consolidation
by the victor. The Gbagbo elite was destroyed or fled — Gbagbo captured April 11,
FDS leaders arrested or fled to exile. Ouattara consolidated power through the
RHDP alliance and FRCI military. Guillaume Soro became PM. Tensions within the
Ouattara coalition between RDR and PDCI components began emerging.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; HRW Cote d'Ivoire 2011

2012.assessment:
Elite cohesion under Ouattara coalition was significant but with managed tensions.
RHDP alliance held RDR (Ouattara's party) and PDCI (Bedie's party) together.
Guillaume Soro (former FN) as National Assembly president. Tensions between
PDCI and RDR over power-sharing manageable. Security sector integration of
former FN fighters created internal elite complexity.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2012

2013.assessment:
Elite cohesion was reasonably stable in 2013. RHDP coalition functioning.
Former enemies (PDCI and FN elements) integrated into the Ouattara government.
Security sector reform creating tensions with former FN commanders wanting
higher positions. No major elite defections.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2013

2014.assessment:
Elite cohesion remained stable in 2014 under Ouattara's dominant coalition.
PDCI-RDR alliance holding for the 2015 election. Soro managing his political
position. Opposition (Gbagbo in ICC, Bedie aligning with Ouattara) fragmented.
No major elite challenges to Ouattara's authority.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2014

2015.assessment:
Elite cohesion was managed effectively for the 2015 election. Bedie (PDCI)
supported Ouattara, ensuring RHDP unity. Opposition boycott meant the election
was uncontested at the elite level. Military mutiny wages were a latent tension
within security sector elites. Post-election winner-takes-all dynamics beginning.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2015

2016.assessment:
Elite cohesion moderately stable in 2016. New constitution created new
positions (Senate) generating competition. Military wage grievances grew into
the January 2017 mutiny. Soro's positioning vis-à-vis Ouattara increasingly
complex. RDR-PDCI tensions beneath the surface as 2020 succession approached.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2016

2017.assessment:
Elite cohesion showed stress in 2017 with the military mutinies revealing
security sector elite discontent. Ouattara resolved disputes through cash
(costly but politically effective). Soro was Senate President showing RHDP
could manage its coalition. Succession question for 2020 creating behind-
the-scenes competition between Soro, Bedie, and Ouattara's potential candidate.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2017

2018.assessment:
Elite cohesion managed but with increasing succession-related competition in 2018.
Ouattara indicated he would not seek a third term, triggering competition among
potential successors. PDCI (Bedie) began distancing from RHDP. Soro positioning
himself as potential presidential candidate. Tensions managed but competition
more visible.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2018

2019.assessment:
Elite cohesion deteriorating in 2019 as succession conflict intensified.
PDCI (Bedie) formally broke with RHDP. Soro resigned as National Assembly
President and went into exile (December 2019 coup charges). Gbagbo acquittal
by ICC in January 2019 reshuffled opposition elite landscape. RHDP now narrowed
to essentially just RDR.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2019

2020.assessment:
Elite cohesion at its most fractured since the civil war in 2020.
Ouattara's third-term reversal (after PM Gon Coulibaly died in July 2020)
triggered complete elite fragmentation: PDCI (Bedie) and FPI (former Gbagbo
allies) boycotted the election; Soro in exile charged with coup attempt.
Ouattara won uncontested but without legitimacy from the opposition elite.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2020

2021.assessment:
Elite cohesion began recovering in 2021. Gbagbo's return from ICC detention
in June 2021 created political realignment — Gbagbo-RHDP dialogue opened.
Bedie-Ouattara reconciliation discussions. December 2021 legislative elections
saw opposition participate. Elite landscape still fragmented but reconciliation
process reducing active conflict.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Reports; qualitative analysis Cote d'Ivoire 2021

2022.assessment: Ouattara RHDP dominant. Gbagbo acquitted by ICC (2019), returned to CIV; FPI/Gbagbo opposition faction. Bédié died 2023. Minor tensions but RHDP dominant.

2023.assessment: RHDP dominant. Bédié died Dec 2023; succession in PDCI. Gbagbo/FPI competing. Ouattara succession planning. Minor tensions functional.

2024.assessment: Ouattara succession question; multiple RHDP contenders for 2025. Opposition organizing. Significant competition managed.

2025.assessment: 2025 elections; competitive multi-party contest. Post-Ouattara/Bédié new political landscape. Significant competition.

sources.citation: ICG

2009.assessment:
Institutional functioning was severely degraded in 2009. The country remained
de facto divided between the government-controlled south and Forces Nouvelles-
controlled north. Constitutional governance was nominal; elections postponed;
courts and legislatures functioned partially at best. V-Dem liberal democracy
index approximately 0.08 (8/100), reflecting the near-failed state conditions.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2009

2010.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained severely degraded in 2010. Presidential
election was held in November 2010 for the first time in 10 years, indicating
some institutional progress. However, Gbagbo's refusal to accept the result
and the subsequent institutional standoff completely undermined constitutional
order by year-end. Constitutional Court (controlled by Gbagbo) invalidated
results from pro-Ouattara regions.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2010

2011.assessment:
Institutional functioning at its lowest in early 2011 during the civil war
(dual governments, fighting in Abidjan). After Ouattara's April 2011 victory,
institutions were rebuilt under the new government. Parliament held; new
constitutional commission working. V-Dem score approximately 0.09 reflecting
the transitional/rebuilding phase.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2011

2012.assessment:
Institutional functioning showed modest improvement to approximately 11/100.
Legislative elections held December 2011 (though boycotted by some opposition).
New National Assembly functioning. Courts operating but lacking independence.
HIPC completion gave formal recognition to new government structures. Still
severely degraded by comparative standards.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2012

2013.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 12/100 in 2013. Gradual improvement
in government functioning. Security sector reform beginning. DDR process for
former combatants. Parliament operating but dominated by the ruling RHDP
coalition. Judiciary remained under executive influence.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2013

2014.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 12/100 in 2014. Ouattara government
established functioning ministries and public services. Independent agencies
(electoral commission CEI) beginning to function. Media regulatory environment
improving. Opposition parties functioning but weakened. Judiciary reform slow.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2014

2015.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 13/100 in 2015. Presidential election
held October 2015 with Ouattara winning 84% but amid opposition boycott.
Electoral process improved from 2010 but lacked genuine competition. Parliament
functional. Security sector partially reformed. Civil society space limited.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2015

2016.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 13/100 in 2016. New constitution
adopted by referendum October 2016, creating a Senate. Constitutional
changes concentrated power in the presidency. Legislative elections in 2016.
Institutional formalism improving while substantive checks and balances weak.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2016

2017.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 14/100 in 2017. Senate created under
new constitution. Military mutinies (Jan and May) challenged government
authority but were resolved without institutional breakdown. ONUCI mandate
ended June 2017. Government now solely responsible for security.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2017

2018.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 14/100 in 2018. Government institutions
consolidating. Constitutional Court functioning but lacking independence. IMF
program required improved institutional governance. Political environment
increasingly consolidated around Ouattara's RHDP dominant party.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2018

2019.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 15/100 in 2019. Modest improvement
with more established institutions. Gbagbo ICC acquittal (January 2019) and
his return question consumed political attention. Electoral commission reform
in preparation for 2020 election. Opposition parties beginning to participate
more actively.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2019

2020.assessment:
Institutional functioning declined to approximately 12/100 in 2020. Ouattara's
third-term candidacy was challenged as unconstitutional; Constitutional Court
approved it. Opposition boycott of October election and post-election violence
revealed institutional fragility. Courts used to enable incumbent's agenda
rather than as independent arbiters.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2020

2021.assessment:
Institutional functioning approximately 13/100 in 2021. Post-election
reconciliation beginning. Gbagbo returned from ICC detention in June 2021,
creating political realignment. Opposition partially normalizing. Parliament
elected December 2021. Institutions functioning at low but stable level.

sources.citation: V-Dem v2x_libdem, Cote d'Ivoire 2021

2022.assessment: Ouattara third term (controversial but stabilized). Institutions functional but flawed; RHDP dominant; opposition weak. Courts function. Some CSO and media space.

2023.assessment: Functional but flawed; Ouattara preparing succession. Parliament and judiciary active. Media partially free.

sources.citation: BTI

2024.assessment: Functional but flawed. Pre-election period; Ouattara succession question. RHDP dominant but competitive elections expected.

sources.citation: BTI

2025.assessment: Presidential elections October 2025. Ouattara reportedly not running; first free and fair competitive succession. Institutions functional but competitive.

2009.assessment:
Political violence was at a low level in 2009, with approximately 300 conflict
deaths. The 2007 Ouagadougou Agreement maintained a fragile peace between the
government and Forces Nouvelles. Sporadic clashes occurred in the zone of
confidence but no major offensive. Political violence linked to ongoing
armed actor competition rather than all-out war.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire conflict events 2009

2010.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 450 in 2010. Sporadic clashes continued
through most of 2010 at low levels. The post-election crisis beginning November
2010 (Gbagbo refusal to concede) dramatically escalated violence in December,
with forces aligned to both Gbagbo and Ouattara clashing. The full toll of the
crisis extended into 2011.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2010; HRW reporting on post-election crisis

2011.assessment:
Political violence deaths were approximately 3,000 in 2011, the worst year since
the 2002-2003 civil war outbreak. The post-election civil war (Jan-Apr 2011)
resulted in FRCI (Ouattara forces) advance, French and ONUCI intervention,
and urban fighting in Abidjan. Approximately 3,000+ were killed including
significant civilian massacres in Duékoué (west) and other areas. War ended
April 2011 with Gbagbo's capture.

sources.citation: HRW 2012 World Report; Amnesty International; UCDP GED 2011

2012.assessment:
Political violence deaths declined sharply to approximately 400 in 2012 as
Ouattara consolidated control. Pro-Gbagbo militia attacks from western Liberia
border area continued to kill civilians and security forces. Some revenge killings
against former Gbagbo supporters. UN peacekeepers (ONUCI) supporting stability.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2012; ONUCI reports

2013.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 200 in 2013. Continuing decline
in violence as security sector stabilized. Sporadic pro-Gbagbo militia
attacks in the west. Intercommunal clashes between farming and herder
communities were the primary remaining security challenge.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2013

2014.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 120 in 2014, the lowest since the
civil war. Security sector reform progressing. Pro-Gbagbo militia significantly
degraded. Intercommunal violence around land tenure remained but declined.
Country achieving meaningful security stabilization.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2014

2015.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 80 in 2015, reaching a post-war low.
Security stabilization successful. Presidential election in October 2015 passed
without significant violence (though opposition boycott meant no competitive
tension). Country achieving genuine stability.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2015

2016.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 90 in 2016. Continued low-level
intercommunal violence around land tenure. Constitutional referendum in
October 2016 did not generate significant violence. Security sector tensions
(military wage grievances) latent.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2016

2017.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 100 in 2017. January and May military
mutinies caused limited deaths; primarily extortion and property damage rather
than killing. Intercommunal violence remained the primary driver. Northern
Sahel jihadist spillover beginning but minimal fatalities.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2017

2018.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 90 in 2018. Intercommunal violence
continued at low level. Northern jihadist activity increasing slightly.
Political environment consolidating under Ouattara. Opposition Gbagbo ICC
trial (acquittal Feb 2019) political attention without violence.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2018

2019.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 150 in 2019. Uptick partly from
northern jihadist attacks (Kafolo June 2020 pre-announced risk) and
intercommunal violence. Gbagbo acquittal by ICC in January 2019 and
subsequent return discussions created political tension without immediate
violence.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2019; ACLED northern incidents

2020.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 500 in 2020 — a significant increase.
Post-election violence (October-December) from Ouattara's third-term candidacy
killed approximately 85+ people directly. Jihadist attacks in the north
(Kafolo June 2020) killed soldiers. Intercommunal violence elevated ahead of
elections. Overall the worst year since 2013.

sources.citation: UCDP GED + ACLED, Cote d'Ivoire 2020; HRW election violence report

2021.assessment:
Political violence deaths approximately 200 in 2021. Post-election violence
from 2020 largely wound down. Northern jihadist attacks continued at elevated
level. Reconciliation process between Ouattara and opposition reducing
political violence driver.

sources.citation: UCDP GED, Cote d'Ivoire 2021

2022.assessment: Minimal violence. Ouattara third term stabilized. Some jihadist attacks in north from Burkina Faso. ~80 conflict deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire 2022

2023.assessment: Low violence. Some jihadist incursions in north increasing deaths. ~120 deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire

2024.assessment: Low violence; jihadist attacks from Burkina in north. Pre-election tensions mild. ~150 deaths from jihadist and criminal violence.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire 2024

2025.assessment: Estimated low violence. 2025 elections create political competition. Jihadist border threat managed.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire

1996.assessment: Bédié government controls full territory. Côte d'Ivoire relatively stable despite rising Ivoirité political tensions.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

1997.assessment: Full control. Political tensions (Ivoirité) but no armed challenge to government authority.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

1998.assessment: Full control. Bédié consolidating; Ivoirité exclusion of Ouattara sharpening.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

1999.assessment: Full control until December 24, 1999: General Guéï coup (CNSP) overthrows Bédié. Brief uncertainty; full national control maintained under junta.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire Coup Analysis

2000.assessment: Guéï elections October 2000; popular uprising ousts Guéï after he tries to steal election; Gbagbo declared winner. Post-election RDR supporter violence (50+ killed). Minor territorial disruption.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire 2000

2001.assessment: Gbagbo government; full territorial control. Forum for National Reconciliation attempted. Underlying tensions but no armed insurgency.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

2002.assessment: September 19, 2002: Military mutiny in Abidjan/Bouaké/Korhogo. Forces Nouvelles (FMCI/MPCI) takes approximately 60% of national territory overnight including all major northern cities. FANCI controls Abidjan and south. Country effectively partitioned.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire: "The War Is Not Yet Over"
UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2003.assessment: Continued civil war partition. Linas-Marcoussis Accords (January 2003) nominally create government of national reconciliation but FN retains north. MPIGO/MJP also active in west. French Licorne and ONUCI deploy zone of confidence. ~60% territory outside FANCI control.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire; Linas-Marcoussis Accords 2003

2004.assessment: Partition continues. November 2004: Gbagbo airstrikes on Bouaké kill 9 French soldiers; Licorne destroys FANCI air force in retaliation; anti-French riots in Abidjan. Country remains de facto divided. Accra III process. ~60% outside government control.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire: No Peace in Sight

2005.assessment: Partition unchanged. FN controls north; FANCI south. ONUCI/Licorne zone of confidence maintained. AU transitional arrangements. Elections postponed. Pretoria Agreement.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

2006.assessment: Continued partition. ONUCI and Licorne maintaining zone of confidence. Various peace initiatives failing. FN controls north.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire

2007.assessment: March 2007: Ouagadougou Political Agreement (OPA). FN leader Guillaume Soro becomes PM; reunification process begins. Zone of confidence dissolves; joint security operations. FN nominally integrating but still de facto controls north. Significant improvement.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire After the OPA

2008.assessment: OPA implementation progressing; DDR and security sector reform. FN nominally integrating but maintaining armed presence in north. Government control gradually expanding. Elections postponed again.

sources.citation: ICG, Côte d'Ivoire 2008

2009.assessment:
Côte d'Ivoire remained de facto divided in 2009 following the 2002-2007 civil
war. The 2007 Ouagadougou Political Agreement (OPA) had nominally unified the
country but the Forces Nouvelles (FN) still controlled approximately 60% of
territory in the north. The government and FN maintained a fragile peace;
ONUCI peacekeepers patrolled the zone of confidence. Elections were repeatedly
postponed. Reunification was nominal rather than substantive.

sources.citation: ONUCI mission reports 2009; UCDP Cote d'Ivoire conflict data

2010.assessment:
The territorial partition continued through 2010. Forces Nouvelles still controlled
the north. The November 2010 presidential election led to a disputed outcome:
Ouattara won internationally recognized victory but Gbagbo refused to concede.
By December, parallel governments operated, with the Forces Nouvelles backing
Ouattara and government forces backing Gbagbo. The country was contested between
these two blocs.

sources.citation: ICG Africa Report 2010; ONUCI reports; UCDP Cote d'Ivoire data

2011.assessment:
2011 was the regime change year. January-April saw the post-election civil war:
Forces Nouvelles (FRCI - Republican Forces of Côte d'Ivoire) under Ouattara
launched offensive from the north while pro-Gbagbo FDS held the south and
Abidjan. UNOCI/French forces intervened to enforce UN mandate. Gbagbo captured
April 11, 2011. By end of 2011 Ouattara forces controlled full national
territory, though disarming pro-Gbagbo militias remained incomplete.

sources.citation: HRW 2012, Cote d'Ivoire Post-Election Violence; ONUCI 2011 reports; UCDP data

2012.assessment:
By 2012 the Ouattara government nominally controlled all territory, but
security remained fragile. Pro-Gbagbo militias (largely from the west/Guéré
communities) conducted sporadic attacks from bases in Liberia. The west
of the country (Moyen-Cavally) remained the most insecure. UN peacekeepers
still deployed.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2012; ICG Africa Briefing 2012

2013.assessment:
Government controlled all territory in 2013. Pro-Gbagbo militia activity
continued at low level in western Côte d'Ivoire, particularly around Tai
and Guiglo. Militia cross-border attacks from Liberia sporadically killed
civilians. Government security forces consolidated control but western
areas remained insecure.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire 2013; UNSC reports S/2013

2014.assessment:
Government controlled all territory in 2014. Western militia activity
declined significantly compared to 2012-2013. Security sector reform
(DDR processes) progressing slowly. Former Forces Nouvelles elements
largely integrated into FRCI national army. Stability improving.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2014; UN ONUCI progress reports

2015.assessment:
Full territorial control in 2015. Ouattara re-elected in October 2015
(opposition boycott). Western security improved further. ONUCI mandate
approaching end. Military mutiny risk was latent but not active. Country
achieving meaningful stability.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2015; ONUCI final reports

2016.assessment:
Full territorial control in 2016. ONUCI mission completed and withdrew
June 2017 (planned withdrawal begun 2016). Military mutiny in January 2016
(actually 2017 Jan) foreshadowed; actually Jan/May 2017 mutinies. Security
sector tensions latent. New constitution adopted October 2016 creating
a Senate.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2016

2017.assessment:
Full territorial control in 2017. January and May military mutinies involved
soldiers seizing barracks in Bouaké and Abidjan demanding bonus payments.
These were wage disputes, not territorial challenges. Government resolved
both mutinies with cash payments. ONUCI mandate ended June 2017.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2017; BBC Africa reporting on mutinies

2018.assessment:
Full territorial control in 2018. Security improved significantly from the
early post-civil war years. Rural west remained somewhat insecure. Political
succession discussions (who would succeed Ouattara in 2020) beginning.
No active territorial challenges.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2018

2019.assessment:
Full territorial control in 2019. Security stable. Ouattara announced he
would not seek third term (later reversed). Sahel jihadist spillover from
Mali/Burkina Faso created emerging threat in northern border areas but
no territorial loss.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2019; ICG Sahel spillover analysis

2020.assessment:
Government controlled full territory in 2020. Post-election violence October-
December 2020 (Ouattara's controversial third-term candidacy) was political and
ethnic violence, not insurgency. Approximately 85 people killed in electoral
violence. Northern jihadist infiltration at low level. Government controlled
all populated areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire 2020; HRW election violence reporting

2021.assessment:
Government controlled full territory in 2021. Post-election political tensions
gradually easing with reconciliation discussions. Jihadist threat from
Sahel continued to grow in northern border areas (Hambol and Poro regions)
but remained minor. Ouattara government consolidated without major security challenge.

sources.citation: ACLED Cote d'Ivoire data 2021; ICG Cote d'Ivoire briefings

2022.assessment: Government controls full territory. Ouattara third term stabilized. Some jihadist spill-over from Burkina Faso in north. Security forces managing border regions.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire

2023.assessment: Full control. Some jihadist incursions from Burkina Faso in north-west (Tchologo region). Security forces responding. Pre-2025 election maneuvering.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire

2024.assessment: Mostly full control. Jihadist spill-over from Burkina increasing in north. Security forces maintaining presence but some communities insecure.

sources.citation: ACLED, Côte d'Ivoire 2024

2025.assessment: Estimated mostly full control. 2025 presidential elections (October) create political competition. Jihadist border threat managed.

2009.assessment:
Budget transparency was very low in 2009. The 2008 Open Budget Survey gave
Cote d'Ivoire a score of approximately 9 out of 100 — scant information
provided to the public. The ongoing political crisis (north-south partition)
severely constrained normal budget processes. Budget documents were not
published publicly and there was no meaningful legislature scrutiny.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Cote d'Ivoire

2010.assessment:
Budget transparency at its lowest in 2010. The OBS 2010 survey gave Cote
d'Ivoire a score of approximately 5 out of 100 — near-total lack of public
budget information. The post-election crisis beginning November 2010 made
normal budgeting operations essentially non-functional by year end.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Cote d'Ivoire

2011.assessment:
Budget transparency essentially non-existent during the 2011 civil war.
The Gbagbo-Ouattara parallel government period meant two rival budget
processes. No public budget documents produced or published. International
financial institutions suspended normal engagement during the conflict.
Using 2010 OBS score as proxy; actual transparency likely even lower
during the combat period.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010; conflict-period qualitative assessment

2012.assessment:
Budget transparency improved significantly from 2011 but remained minimal.
The 2012 OBS gave Cote d'Ivoire a score of approximately 17 out of 100.
Post-conflict rebuilding of budget institutions under Ouattara government
with IMF/World Bank support. HIPC completion in June 2012 improved budget
reporting requirements. Some budget documents now published but public
access still very limited.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Cote d'Ivoire

2013.assessment:
Budget transparency remained at minimal levels in 2013. No OBS survey
year — interpolated from 2012 survey results. Continued improvements in
budget process under IMF ECF program conditionality. National Development
Plan 2012-2015 providing framework for improved public finance management,
but public access to detailed budget information remained limited.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012 (interpolated); IMF ECF program reports

2014.assessment:
Budget transparency at minimal levels in 2014, with gradual improvements.
Non-OBS survey year — interpolated between 2012 and 2015 surveys. TOFE
(financial operations table) began to be published more regularly. World
Bank PEFA assessment and IMF program driving improvements in public financial
management. Citizens' budget not yet published.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2014; IMF/WB PFM reform program

2015.assessment:
Budget transparency remained minimal in 2015. OBS 2015 survey gave Cote
d'Ivoire a score of approximately 18 out of 100. Progress was slow despite
IMF and World Bank public financial management reform programs. Côte d'Ivoire
lagged behind regional peers like Ghana and Senegal. Budget documents are
produced but not widely disseminated or accessible to citizens.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Cote d'Ivoire

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency at minimal levels in 2016. Non-OBS survey year —
interpolated between 2015 and 2017 surveys. Budget reform commitments
continued under National Development Plan 2016-2020. Some improvements
in executive budget publication but legislative scrutiny and audit
functions remained weak.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2016; NDP 2016-2020 PFM commitments

2017.assessment:
Budget transparency improved marginally to approximately 19 out of 100 in
2017 OBS survey. Still in the minimal information category. More budget
documents available but quality and accessibility limited. Citizens' budget
not published. In-year budget reports published with delays. Audit institution
(IGF) reports not publicly available.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Cote d'Ivoire

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency at minimal/low levels in 2018. Non-OBS survey year —
interpolated between 2017 and 2019 surveys, with trajectory of modest
improvement. Publication of budget documents improving under PEFA assessment
follow-up. Some progress on executive budget proposal and enacted budget
publication.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2018; PEFA assessment follow-up

2019.assessment:
Budget transparency improved to approximately 27 out of 100 in the 2019 OBS
survey — entering the lower end of "minimal" category. Significant improvement
from 2009 baseline of 9. Executive budget proposal now being published publicly.
Enacted budget and year-end report improvements noted. Citizens' budget still
absent. Legislature scrutiny improving but audit function weak.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Cote d'Ivoire

2020.assessment:
Budget transparency at minimal levels in 2020. Non-OBS survey year —
using 2019 survey score as proxy. COVID-19 emergency budget measures
and supplemental budgets were enacted but transparency of emergency
spending was limited. Election-year political pressures did not improve
budget openness.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2019 (proxy); COVID-19 budget supplementals

2021.assessment:
Budget transparency improved to approximately 31 out of 100 in the 2021 OBS
survey — continued gradual progress. Still in the "minimal" category but
trending toward "limited." More budget documents publicly available than
in any previous period. Mid-year reviews improving. Legislature scrutiny
function strengthening. Audit reports not yet fully public.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021, Cote d'Ivoire

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership OBI Survey Cote d'Ivoire

2024.assessment: Budget transparency score approximately 46/100 in 2024. Estimated slight improvement; continued institutional development

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership OBI Survey Cote d'Ivoire

2025.assessment: Budget transparency score approximately 47/100 in 2025. Projected gradual improvement in budget transparency
